[[RunningHead]]

University Forum: Revitalizing the South Side of Chicago

Gabriel Ruiz

Gabriel Ruiz

111 North Wabash Avenue, Suite 1319
Chicago, IL 60602
E-mail: g@briel.me

Gabriel Ruiz  

The June 2016 University Forum included Janet L. Smith, Associate Professor and Co-Director of the Voorhies Center at the University of Illinois at Chicago, and Angela Hurlock, an architect and Executive Director of Claretian Associates, with Robert Galatzer-Levy as moderator. Laurence Ralph, John L. Loeb Associate Professor of the Social Sciences at Harvard University, could not attend but provided his paper for this report. Although the panel, held at the APsaA meeting in Chicago, wrestled with questions particular to that city, participants addressed issues equally relevant to other major cities across the country: How to revitalize inner city areas like the South Side of Chicago that suffer from underdevelopment and lack of resources, leaving their populations underserved and impoverished? Further, can psychoanalysis help? If so, how?

A basic requirement for revitalizing a community is providing for the safety of its residents. A UNICEF statement in 2016 noted that “despite intellectual debates about the definition of childhood and cultural differences about what to expect of children, there has always been a substantial degree of shared understanding that childhood implies a separated and safe space.” Thus, within their respective cultures, children require a safe environment, free from violence and trauma, for their play and education, as well as for social connections and healthy attachments.

Patrick Bracken (2002) offers us a model for thinking about how people function within communities. Bracken states that all of us function in the world, whatever our cultural context, because we have a sense of “underlying coherence” that provides meaning through an expectable, organizing way of establishing early patterns, which get folded deeply into ourselves. As psychoanalysts we can contribute our understanding of how environmental affordances and traumas impact one's sense of self and can offer evidence that the processes of internalization are largely unconscious.

More Courtship Letters of Freud and Martha Bernays

Sigmund Freud, Martha Bernays: Die Brautbriefe, Band 3. “Warten in Ruhe und Ergebung, Warten in Kampf und Erregung” (The Letters during Their Engagement, Vol. 3. “Waiting Quietly and With Surrender, Waiting Struggling and With Exasperation”). Edited by Gerhard Fichtner, Ilse Grubrich-Simitis, Albrecht Hirschmüller, and Wolfgang Kloft. Frankfurt a.M.: S. Fischer Verlag, 2015, 620 pp., €49.40.

Rita Teusch

Rita Teusch

129 Mt. Auburn Strreet
Cambridge, MA 12138
E-mail: ritateusch@verizon.net

Rita Teusch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Instructor in Psychiatry (Psychology), part-time, Cambridge Health Alliance, Harvard Medical School.

This third volume (of a projected five) of the unabridged courtship correspondence (more than 1500 letters in all) between Freud and his wife-to-be Martha Bernays contains 306 of their letters written over a period of eight months between January and September 1884, eighteen months into their four-year separation while engaged. The subtitle of this book is a quote from one of Freud's letters (732F) in which he refers to an earlier letter from Bernays (729B).1 In this letter Freud eloquently and poignantly describes and analyzes their different temperaments, the history of their involvement, and how he has grown more patient and now appreciates Bernays's emotional independence and her ability to say no to him at times. The different modes of “waiting” described in the quote refer to the complexity of their internal struggles with “waiting for the other” and highlight their differences in temperament, with Bernays overall being more inclined to wait patiently and with surrender, whereas Freud could experience waiting as an exasperating internal struggle projected onto her during times of increased stress.

1Letters will be identified throughout by the number assigned them by the editors, with F or B identifying Freud or Bernays as the writer. All translations mine.111

Response to Commentaries

Pumla Gobodo-Madikizela

Pumla Gobodo-Madikizela

Private Bag X1
Matieland 7602
SOUTH AFRICA
E-mail: pumlagm1@gmail.com

Pumla Gobodo-Madikizela  

I wish to thank the editors of JAPA for inviting five colleagues to comment on my paper, “Psychological Repair: The Intersubjective Dialogue of Remorse and Forgiveness in the Aftermath of Gross Human Rights Violations” (JAPA 63/6, 2015). I can hardly begin to do justice to their thoughtful comments, but I will do my best to address the issues they raise, and to explore how the different perspectives discussed in their papers might connect with some of the ideas I have been trying to develop in my work. My earlier discussions of forgiveness (e.g., Gobodo-Madikizela 2003) tried to articulate forgiveness as a response to a remorseful apology and presented both forgiveness and remorse as implying the “rehumanization” of both victims and perpetrators. In my recent work, I turn to the pivotal issue of intersubjectivity in the context of victim-perpetrator dialogue, and explore how the concepts playing a central role in the forgiveness response—acknowledgment, recognition, remorse, openness, forgiveness—work in practice and how they enable change and transformation.

Given the great relevance of the issues raised in the commentaries, I should, before responding, mention briefly what has happened in South Africa in the year since my paper appeared. The dramatic unfolding of events in all sectors of our society, including the turmoil in the governing African National Congress, exemplified, among other things, by a major investigation into the looting of government coffers, what has been termed “state capture,”1 led me to focus sharply on the question of what is missing in the reconciliation discourse.

On the twentieth anniversary of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC), black students' protests, which began at previously

1The official report on the investigation into alleged improper and unethical conduct by President Jacob Zuma and other government officials can be found at: http://saflii.org/images/329756472-State-of-Capture.pdfResearch Chair, Studies in Historical Trauma and Transformation, Stellenbosch University, South Africa.

Matthew Shaw   Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis and Yale School of Medicine.

When I was in college, a popular professor of postmodernism wrote widely about the death of the father. With a dazzling command of Foucault, Derrida, and Baudrillard, along with their modern predecessors including Freud, he showed how various authorities were made and maintained rather than given. He exposed the many ways in which realities are constructed and can therefore be deconstructed. And yet, as he vigorously discussed the death of the father, he did so in what I thought was a paternalistic way. Though his words asserted that the father was dead, his charismatically authoritative stance suggested that a new father had come to campus and was as vital as ever. His approach represented for me both the potency and the seductiveness of some postmodern writing.

Upon reading that S. Montana Katz's edited volume, Metaphor and Fields, was an attempt to address “an era in which psychoanalysis is coming to terms with the conclusions and implications of postmodernism” (p. 4), I worried that it would be full of postmodern pyrotechnics and be more clever than sound. I was wrong. Katz has convened an assembly of rigorous, thoughtful writers who are wrestling with fundamental questions about psychoanalysis as a discipline and psychic experience as our area of study. She ambitiously attempts to find what is irreducible in order to facilitate communication between and beyond analysts, even when such interaction merely clarifies differences.

The book's first section builds on the ideas presented by George Lakoff and Mark Johnson in Metaphors We Live By (1980). In their painstaking study, Lakoff and Johnson identify and analyze the most common metaphors associated with hundreds of concepts. Soul Machine: The Invention of the Modern Mind. By George Makari. New York: Norton, 2015, xvi + 656 pp., $39.95 hardcover, $19.95 paperback.

Dale Boesky

Dale Boesky

614 Watkins Road
Birmingham, Ml 48009

Dale Boesky   Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; former Editor-in-Chief, Psychoanalytic Quarterly.

Before 1492 it took a bold imagination to envision what the world would look like if it were a sphere. It is similarly difficult to imagine what the mind would look like if there were no concept of mind. Thanks to George Makari, we are helped in this remarkable volume to realize again that the emergence of the very notion of “mind” in our civilization was not the consequence of “sedate academic debate” but was instead attended by horrific conflict, often violent debates culminating in the ending of careers if not lives; indeed, though the struggle is less dramatic in modern times, the matter is by no means settled. At least by the time of Bishop Berkeley, it was possible to refer to the conundrums of reality versus Platonic idealism with his famous quip “What is mind, no matter. What is matter, never mind.”

Makari's previous book, Revolution in Mind, has been widely and justly acclaimed for its history of the Freudian era. In this next book, Makari presents a prequel to that first volume, offering his account of the Scottish and British and then French and German Enlightenment, from Thomas Hobbes to Immanuel Kant.

Reading this book I was reminded of the pitfalls of the choice in English of a reifying noun (“mind”) to denote the aggregate of all our mental functioning. Thoughts, of course, aren't actually in our minds. Thoughts (if we were linguistically and logically consistent) would reflect our “minding” functions rather than reified pebbles in a box we call “mind” (for a discussion of this general point of view, see White 1938). The same fallacy in the psychoanalytic world has clouded our use of a noun, the “ego” (and its functions), and led to numerous reminders in our literature that we must guard against reification of “the” ego.

In his magisterial Justice for Hedgehogs, the philosopher Ronald Dworkin introduces two principles that he regards as fundamental requisites for living well. They are, first, self-respect—taking “the objective importance” of one's own life seriously—and, second, authenticity— accepting responsibility for identifying “what counts as success” in one's life and for creating that life “through a coherent narrative or style that he himself endorses. Together the two principles offer a conception of human dignity: dignity requires self-respect and authenticity” (Dworkin 2011, pp. 203-204).

Though psychoanalysis is not one of his concerns, Dworkin incidentally suggests some of the ways in which dignity matters to psychoanalysts: our patients come to us often lacking self-respect, or engaged in behavior that damages it; seemingly unaware that this is their one life, to live and to make to the best of their ability; blaming others for what is their own responsibility, to the detriment of their capacity for autonomy; blind to the ways in which their dignity has been or is being compromised; aping the behavior of others, so not forging their own way of life and values; infringing the integrity of others. One of a psychoanalyst's most important tasks is to help her patient—in Dworkin's words—create a life “through a coherent narrative … that he himself endorses.”

Yet we have given the matter of dignity little explicit attention, Susan S. Levine claims, an oversight she sets out to remedy in this small but inspiring volume of ten essays by psychoanalysts and others. The essays begin by posing theoretical questions about the meaning of dignity and its place in human life, and move on to more clinical considerations.

This book's intriguing title links psychoanalysis, Homer's epic of heroic adventure, and, oddly, painted guinea pigs. The moment's surprise gets

159

explained quickly, but the effect is genial, implying fascination, challenge, and pleasure to come—a prospect the book indeed fulfills.

Guinea pigs figure in a nursery school child's question when, after his class's beloved pet “Guinny” died, their teacher, a few days later, brought a new one in, somewhat different in color. Puzzled by Guinny's death and struggling to understand, the little boy asked, “Who painted him?” The incident focuses attention on how children of that age might manage such a loss, or manage not to. The youngster and his mates took in a puzzling, saddening change but not, as yet, death. So Guinny must have been painted. And thus we join an odyssey of development and inaccessibility, discovery and fear, self-protection and exploration, realized experientially by child and adult analysands and their analysts, alert to the mind's complex workings and wonders, and each mind's uniqueness.

Mahon's book engages as it explains, guides one lucidly through basics of psychoanalytic thought, and illustrates with arresting clinical examples. It offers paths—or in the author's odyssean metaphor, ports of call—through such central psychoanalytic dynamics as repression, transference, play, working through, dreams, screen memories, symptoms, psychological character, and sublimation: the last exemplified by Freud's mourning his father's death, interpreting his own and others' dreams, and formulating the oedipus complex. Yet the book does not read like a textbook. Its prose is sprightly. Fresh perspectives emerge. Mahon writes of insights (whether the patient's or the analyst's), for example, being transgressive, because they undermine defenses that have been relied on. Clinically, therefore, even after having been accepted as true, they often prove forgettable. Established defenses, after all, don't crumble easily. He draws on a patient's image of re-pressing record discs to expand appreciation of the return of the repressed. By insistently re-pressing, repressed impulses command attention, potentially inviting reconsideration. And that dynamic contributes at once to the patient's sense of ongoing burden and inner continuity, to the treatment's difficulty and to its opportunity. Mahon's exposition, in other words, takes the reader on a journey that instructs, complicates, and augments. It proves lively and nuanced, resists lazy certainties or clichés. Often too it affords imaginative access to the actual experience of analysis both for the analysand and for the analyst.

Taken together, these three books cover a wide swath of the history of psychoanalytic ideas and technique. More than that, they address the fundamental principles of contemporary psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice, with attention to the nuanced details involved. The books separately and collectively challenge the reader to question and think about the very foundations of psychoanalysis, as well as its everyday practice, together with the ethical concerns that go along with them. The three books convey the importance, indeed the urgency, of the theoretical and clinical issues facing contemporary psychoanalysis.

Robin Brown, in Psychoanalysis beyond the End of Metaphysics: Thinking towards the Post-Relational, canvasses, in an impressively small number of pages and in sentences at times perhaps too condensed, both a large amount of Western philosophy and the history of modern psychiatry and psychoanalysis. Consciousness, that most immediate dimension of human experience, remains the most mysterious dimension of our being. Even saying clearly what constitutes the experience of consciousness remains elusive despite enormous philosophical efforts. Demonstrating its presence in anyone but oneself requires a leap of faith. Machines' ever increasing capacity to behave as if conscious through mechanisms that do not require actual consciousness has led to wide-ranging considerations, from philosophy to science fiction. Attempts to explain the mechanisms of consciousness have baffled the best scientific minds. Following Descartes' replacement of the soul with the conscious mind, the capacity for consciousness became the essence of being human. This carries enormous moral and ethical implications. Are people to be held responsible for actions taken when they are not conscious or in an altered state of consciousness? In the absence of ordinary consciousness can a person be said to be alive, and to what extent should a body that may have the potential for ordinary consciousness be preserved in the hope that consciousness will return?

John Forrester died in November 2015, and was working on Thinking in Cases until near the end of his life. In her preface to the book, his widow, Lisa Appignanesi, mentions that she compiled the final chapter from a lecture and his notes. “The sources he brought to bear were always vast,” she writes. “Mimicking his agility in argument, let alone imagining a position he might arrive at, is a little like playing chess with a master when you barely know the rules of the game” (p. x). Adam Phillips comments in his introduction that Forrester “seemed to have read everything” (p. xiii). Forrester was indeed a polymath, and the scope of his erudition presented a challenge for me, as I imagine it will for many readers. This is a book that requires careful reading.

The initial challenge the reader faces is that, with the exception of Forrester's opening chapter, each essay in the book deals with a particular “case,” with extensive reference to a background text. Some of these texts I had read, and some I had not. For example, The Structure of Scientific Revolutions is the central text cited in chapter 2, “On Kuhn's Case: Psychoanalysis and the Paradigm.” My own reading of Kuhn's classic work was too far in the past to fully inform a present reading of Forrester's chapter. Should I then first read or re-read these background texts, or press on without doing so? Of necessity I pressed on.

This is a book for our times, as the introductory references to Barak Obama's Dreams from My Father and Steve McQueen's film Twelve Years a Slave suggest. Volney Gay's analysis of the psychic pleasures and costs of slavery is informed by growing up lower-class in Portland, Oregon, where he learned what it feels like to be lesser than. Only later did he realize the many psychic pleasures he enjoyed by being white. As he and others have explored, white privilege is largely invisible to those who possess it. Because whites have always had it, because they do not know what its absence feels like, they accept their pleasurable superiority as natural. They are unaware how oppressive their assumptions can feel to people of color.

By exploring the origin of these pleasures in the institution of slavery, Gay has several aims, the largest of which is to encourage us to have empathy—even with slave owners. He wants us to acknowledge that in their place we might have acted as they did (and the “they” here is inclusive: he cites instances of free black masters who “like white owners … sold slaves when they needed funds or when they were dissatisfied with their slaves’ behavior” (p. 93). By acknowledging our capacity to oppress others, by refusing to demonize the slaveholder, we remain open to understanding.

Sigmund Freud (1926a)

Karl Abraham's life and work stand as a seminal chapter in the early history of psychoanalysis. He was closely connected to other important figures in our field, whose lives intersected with his: patients, colleagues, and collaborators, as well as competitors vis-à-vis Freud and occasionally outright adversaries. His pioneering work influenced the lives and work of analysts including Melanie Klein, Sándor Radó, Alix and James Strachey, Karen Horney, the Glover brothers, Helene and Felix Deutsch, Bertram Lewin (Thompson 2010), and Edith Jacobson. Given the contemporary interest in building connections between Kleinians, British Middle School followers, object relations theorists, and ego psychologists, we can trace commonalities back to the analyst-teacher Abraham, as well as divergences as they unfolded.

Before this biography of Abraham, little was available in English. We had the oft-quoted obituary by Ernest Jones (1926), a chapter by Martin Grotjahn in the volume Psychoanalytic Pioneers (1966), an incomplete biographical sketch by Abraham's daughter, the psychoanalyst Hilde Abraham (1974), Abraham's Clinical Papers and Essays on Psychoanalysis (1955), the Letters of Sigmund Freud and Karl Abraham (Abraham and Reud 1965), The Complete Correspondence of Sigmund Freud and Karl Abraham (Falzeder 2002), and a handful of papers on Abraham. One of those papers is Serge Lebovici's presidential address to the International Psychoanalytical Association in Jerusalem in 1977 (Lebovici 1978) on the centenary of Abraham's birth. That paper praises his contributions to theory and practice and reminded the audience of Abraham's leadership role in the IPA. But that address was given almost forty years ago.

Anna Bentinck van Schoonheten, in preparing this biography of Abraham, was able to access sources previously unavailable, especially the complete letters of Abraham to Freud (see a review of the English translation by Axel Hoffer [2004]). These letters were apparently buried among the papers of the Eitingon estate in Israel. Van Schoonheten also consulted primary and secondary sources available only in German (e.g., Cremerius 1999). Her portrayal of Abraham and his world is presented in chronological sequence, as she carefully delineates factual details apart from her dynamic speculations. She builds a portrait of Abraham the person and family man, interwoven with his writings and professional activities. As she provides specific details about all that is known about Abraham, she gives readers the opportunity to make their own conclusions alongside the author.

Patricia Everett, a practicing psychologist and serious scholar, had been immersed in the study of the life and work of Mabel Dodge Luhan for many years when she came across a packet of letters from A. A. Brill to Luhan at Yale's Beinecke Library. Everett's first book (ten years in the making), A History of Having a Great Many Times Not Continued to Be Friends, documents the correspondence between Luhan and Gertrude Stein. In her introduction to the book under review here, Everett writes of her thrill in discovering the letters from Brill to Luhan: “For over fifteen years, I periodically transcribed and annotated the existing letters from Abraham Arden Brill to Mabel Dodge Luhan, archived in a formerly restricted file at the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library at Yale University. I longed for Mabel's side of the correspondence, but resigned myself to Brill's daughter's assertions [that the letters had been destroyed]… . then, unexpectedly, in 2004, Mabel's letters to Brill, as well as eleven additional ones from Brill to her, were discovered among unsorted material during a recataloguing of the Luhan collection. A miraculous escape from the fire thus gave rise to this book of correspondence” (pp. xxiii-xxiv). By now the reader will have gathered the extent of Everett's passion for her subject, and her persistence.

Luhan was a woman of letters whose home in Taos, New Mexico, and apartment at 23 Fifth Avenue in New York became vital centers for those in the arts. Her New York salon was a gathering place for the literati and avant-garde, and the venue for her introduction to Brill and for Brill's introduction of psychoanalysis to America.1 The book contains all the correspondence between the two parties that is extant: forty-six letters from Mabel to Brill, and seventy-six from Brill to Mabel. The book presents not a mere transcription of the letters but a narrative organized around them, with passages providing background and context.

Sidney Blatt's Psychoanalytic Legacy: An Introduction

Golan Shahar and Linda Mayes

Linda Mayes

800 Ben-Gurion Avenue
Beer-Sheva 84105
ISRAEL
E-mail: shaharg@bgu.ac.il

Linda Mayes  

This special section of articles is dedicated to elucidating the psychoanalytic legacy of Sidney Blatt (1928-2014). Known for his many contributions to psychoanalysis, personality/developmental/clinical psychology, and psychotherapy research, Blatt was a formidable bridge builder across disciplines and perspectives. His ability to integrate theory, empirical research, and clinical practice rendered his work a source of inspiration for scholars and clinicians who value a psychodynamic perspective.

Those of us close to Sid Blatt are keenly aware of his fierce commitment to psychoanalysis. He was committed not just to the intellectual edifice and the method of treatment, but also to the intellectual and professional movement that began with Freud in Vienna and spread throughout the world. We are also aware that such commitment did not always come easy to Sid. He was acutely cognizant of the movement's and the treatment's limitations and flaws, chief of which was, in his opinion, psychoanalysts' ambivalence toward empirical research. But Sid bore this tension with characteristic dignity and grace, and he used to posit that this tension reflects the human situation: all things are multilayered, and people and ideas are complex, sometimes seemingly contradictory. One must bear this, he thought, and do what one can to sort through (and respect) the complexity.

It was Sid's inspiring ability to tolerate, even “play with,” tension and complexity, juxtaposed with his prodigious intellectual abilities, that enabled him to make his decades-long, diverse contribution to psychoanalytic theory, research, and clinical practice. Fortunately, for many, he did so by sharing his gifts with students and colleagues.

Golan Shahar, Professor of Clinical-Health Psychology, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Israel. Linda C. Mayes, Director, Yale Child Study Center.

Mental Representation in the Thought of Sidney Blatt: Developmental Processes

John Auerbach and Diana Diamond

Diana Diamond

7697 SW 24th Lane
Gainesville, FL 32608-0328
E-mail: auerbachjohn@yahoo.com

Diana Diamond  

mental representation, developmental processes, unconscious processes, attachment, intersubjectivity, internalization, cognitive morphology, level of representation, mother-infant relationship, Object Relations Inventory, Conceptual Level Scale, Differentiation-Relatedness Scale

Mental representation was a central construct in Sidney Blatt's contributions to psychology and psychoanalysis. This brief review demonstrates that Blatt's understanding of representation was always informed by basic psychoanalytic concepts like the centrality of early caregiver-infant relationships and of unconscious mental processes. Although Blatt's earlier writings were informed by psychoanalytic ego psychology and Piagetian cognitive developmental psychology, they focused nonetheless on how an individual uses bodily and relational experiences to construct an object world; they also consistently presented object representations as having significant unconscious dimensions. From the mid-1980s onward, Blatt's contributions, in dialogue with his many students, moved in an even more experiential/relational direction and manifested the influence of attachment theory, parent-infant interaction research, and intersubjectivity theory. They also incorporated contemporary cognitive psychology, with its emphasis on implicit or procedural, rather than explicit, dimensions as a means of accounting for aspects of object representations that are not in conscious awareness. Throughout his career, however, Blatt regarded mental representation as the construct that mediates between the child's earliest bodily and relational experiences and the mature adult's symbolic, most emotionally profound capacities.

Fire in the Dragon and other Psychoanalytic Essays on Folklore. By Géza Róheim. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1992, xxvi + 194 pp.An Introduction to the Psychology of Fairy Tales. Robert White

Robert White

64 Trumbull Street
New Haven, CT 06510
E-mail: robertswhite@comcast.net

Robert White   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale School of Medicine; faculty, Western New England Psychoanalytic Institute.

Fairy tales have a raw power to transport the hero or heroine out of everyday life into an enchanted space of transformation. Such crossing is frequently prompted by a problem—what to do with an unwanted child; how to find a wife; who will succeed to the throne—but it may also be an adventure of a more transgressive fear or seeking of forbidden knowledge: incest, abandonment, or murder. The magical space may be located in the outer world or in secret interior places: the woods, the depths of the earth, etc. Within this quest, however, primitive and raw emotions are encountered and mastered, and the hero or heroine emerges having grown psychically and socially. Forbidden and repressed conflicts (involving, e.g., incest, cannibalism, envy, sibling rivalry, sexuality) are taken up and worked through for resolution and psychic growth.

Oral folk tales are found in every human culture, and they go back a very long time—probably almost as long as language has existed. Written fairy tales, which derive from the oral folk tradition, appeared in Western Europe in the Middle Ages, blossoming first in Italy, then in France, and finally in Germany.

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

13 Gikhon Street
Jerusalem 9354713
ISRAEL

Rachel Blass  

A view prevails that holding multiple models enhances analytic work, enriches our understanding of pathology, offers different methods of working to meet the needs of a range of patients, and reflects openness to other approaches and the complexity of reality. Against this view it is argued, based on an examination of the nature of analytic models, that because they present comprehensive, divergent conceptions of the person and of analysis, their integration in the context of clinical work, or any shifting between them, is impossible. Although under certain circumstances multiple models seem to be held, in fact this is not so. Adherence to one's commitment to a single model facilitates, rather than constricts, openness to reality and awareness of its complexity.

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

875 Massachusetts Avenue, Suite 54
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven Cooper  

Two clinical vignettes demonstrate a methodological approach that guides the analyst's attention to metaphors and surfaces that are the focus of different theories. Clinically, the use of different theories expands the metaphorical language with which the analyst tries to make contact with the patient's unconscious life. Metaphorical expressions may be said to relate to each other as the syntax of unconscious fantasy (Arlow 1979). The unconscious fantasy itself represents a metaphorical construction of childhood experience that has persisted, dynamically expressive and emergent into adult life. This persistence is evident in how, in some instances, long periods of an analysis focus on translating one or a few metaphors, chiefly because the manifest metaphorical expressions of a central theme regularly lead to better understanding of an unconscious fantasy. At times employing another model or theory assists in a level of self-reflection about clinical understanding and clinical decisions. The analyst's choice of theory or theories is unique to the analyst and is not prescriptive, except as illustrating a way to think about these issues. The use of multiple models in no way suggests or implies that theories may be integrated.

Lucy LaFarge

239 Central Park West, Suite 1-BE

Lucy LaFarge  

In our “post-pluralistic era” (Cooper 2015), analysts, perhaps particularly in North America, are exposed to a multiplicity of formal theories in their training and their reading; their clinical work often reflects ideas drawn from more than one of them. An aspect of this development is the analyst's mental process as she draws on heterogeneous models when with the patient and when processing the events of the session afterward. A number of questions arise here: How does a new piece of clinical understanding, representing an alternative theoretical perspective—one that is not usually at the center of that analyst's thinking—enter the mind of the working analyst? How can the analyst assess whether this is a useful piece of understanding? How can the new piece become assimilated in the analyst's broader thinking? That is, how can the analyst's practice influence her theory? (Canestri 2006).

There are a number of introductory textbooks on psychoanalysis in the marketplace. Among the earliest, Freud's A General Introduction to Psychoanalysis and New Introductory Lectures on Psychoanalysis and Brenner's An Elementary Textbook of Psychoanalysis come to mind. In some respects, these early authors faced a less challenging task in writing a definitive textbook on psychoanalysis than have their successors, insofar as the field, theoretical challenges from dissidents notwithstanding, could still be presented as offering a unified theory of the mind and how it works. Such a monolithic view of psychoanalysis no longer exists, and more recent authors have had to contend with a growing theoretical pluralism in psychoanalysis, as well as with findings from neuropsychology, cognitive science, empirical psychoanalytic research, and psychotherapy process and outcome studies. Today, any book that seeks to be considered a definitive textbook on the subject of psychoanalysis cannot ignore findings from these intersecting fields, and this makes writing such a book a remarkable challenge.

A number of books are self-identified as textbooks, such as a series of excellent volumes written by Glen Gabbard, including Gabbard, Litowitz, and Williams's Textbook of Psychoanalysis, now in its second edition (2011). Other books are adopted as textbooks owing to their clarity for educators and students. In 1994 Nancy McWilliams published the now widely popular Psychoanalytic Diagnosis, which made its way onto the syllabus of numerous introductory courses in psychotherapy. To this list we can add Elizabeth Auchincloss's The Psychoanalytic Model of the

This small but wide-ranging volume by the Argentinian psychoanalyst Julio Moreno deserves careful attention from anyone interested in the human condition, theory of mind, or contemporary culture. The author has an unusual background, having begun in medicine, then moving to research in biophysics and neurophysiology, and finally returning to his early interest in psychology to train in psychiatry, psychoanalysis, and child analysis. This book, which draws on sociology, anthropology, computer science, genetics, philosophy, and psychoanalysis, reflects these diverse interests. Moreno's unique synthesis testifies to his highly original mind. Because his earlier works have appeared almost entirely in Spanish, English-speaking readers have until now had limited access to his ideas and their evolution.

In Treating the Other Third: Vicissitudes of Adolescent Development and Therapy, H. Spencer Bloch, a certified child, adolescent, and adult

571

psychoanalyst, has endeavored to write a clinically serviceable, easy-to-read book regarding adolescent development bolstered by relevant research in the field—in short, a clinical textbook. This primer and his previous book, Adolescent Development, Psychopathology, and Treatment, from 1995, were developed as resources for teaching analytic candidates and psychiatric residents.

The book is organized in three parts and contains five chapters. The organization allows easy navigation of the book's offerings. Part I, the first two chapters, focuses primarily on psychotherapeutic treatment of severe psychopathology as illustrated by two case studies. Bloch informs us of his discovery, in reviewing the literature before writing the book, that fully one-third of adolescents treated for serious psychiatric disorders do not respond to psychotropic medications: hence the book's title. Flat-out refusal to take medication, poor response, and/or unbearable side effects leave fully one-third of teenagers untreatable with psychopharmacological agents. The cases presented reflect this cohort of adolescent patients. Bloch explores internal conflicts related to the presenting problems and the challenges confronting the therapist in maintaining a workable therapeutic frame. Careful consideration of the normative developmental press for independence and emancipation is emphasized.

Parts II and III stand in marked contrast to Part I. These pages unfortunately deal with a controversial and by now recognizably dated approach to homosexuality, one the psychiatric/psychoanalytic field has repudiated. Bloch informs us in his introduction that he tried for eight years to get this book published. I can only wonder if these three chapters did not dissuade publishers from accepting the book. Many readers will be as upset as I was to read, with pain for Bloch's patients and the LGBTQ community, of his untroubled retrogressive alliance with old-fashioned treatment goals in advocating his patients' “conversion” to heterosexuality; as if still in the 1950s, he sees this as a mentally “healthier” option. I feel that his clinical descriptions in Part I are helpful to contemporary practice and would like to give praise where it is due. Any readers curious about the details of Bloch's discredited approach to understanding and working with the LGBTQ community are free to read the chapters and make up their own minds.

Bruce Fink is a distinguished author, translator, and practitioner of Lacanian psychoanalysis. In this recent work he explores concepts of love in psychoanalysis, philosophy, and literature, with an emphasis on the theories of Freud and Lacan. His wide-ranging approach takes up questions of desire, passion, infatuation, sexuality, durable love, and their cultural and historical variations. Along the way, he comments on popular culture and clinical material to convey the myths and mysteries of these universal human experiences. Fink does not attempt a comprehensive review of the subject, omitting, for example, Bergmann's important work on repetition and regression in relationships (1982, 1988) and Kernberg's object relations approach to love (1998). Nor does he cover the varied formulations suggested by Lacan over his long career. Fink focuses principally on Lacan's reading of Plato's Symposium in his Seminar VIII, in which he develops a model of transference love.

Both Freud and Lacan had a lot of things to say about love and its role in psychoanalysis. Freud famously discussed love triangles, the tension between object love and narcissism, the separation of sexual from tender feelings, and the residues of oedipal conflicts in intimate relationships. In his early period, Lacan replaced libidinal motives with the phenomenological notion of a desire that cannot be reduced to drives or object relations, but is inherent in intersubjectivity. “Man's desire is the desire of the Other,” he wrote, borrowing from Hegel's ambiguous phrase. To imitate or fulfill the Other's desire, however, evokes a childish wish for union, not the limited satisfactions of an actual relationship with another subject. In this regard, Lacan distinguished between imaginary love, seeking fusional mirroring of an ideal self, and symbolic love, which accepts incompleteness and separation.

Rachel Hurst, Professor of Womens' and Gender Studies at St. Francis Xavier University in Montreal, has written an exciting book of particular interest to those of us fascinated by the role of the body in psychic life. Right away, one is intrigued with her chapter titles: chapter 1, “Surface

373

Imaginations”; chapter 2, “From the Quest for Recognition to Surface Imagination Surgery”; chapter 3, “The Photograph as Reminder, Evidence, and Promise”; and chapter 4, “The Feminine Skin as Anxious Archipelago.” A fifth chapter, “Conclusion,” follows.

Hurst's thesis is of the moment, especially as it recognizes the impact of cyberspace on the modern psyche. The research is qualitative. She starts by interviewing at length a small sample of people who have had cosmetic surgery. They talk vividly about their bodily concerns and personal bodily aesthetics. She uses conventional narrative when the subject talks in a linear way, with little contradiction in time or event. But when the subject “collapses temporal boundaries” or becomes contradictory, Hurst contracts these long conversations in a highly original fashion. She creates a unique, condensed text crafted solely of the interviewee's own words and ideas that are repeated or stressed. She looks for the person's own reflections, as well as for direct metaphors or affect-laden words. These texts read like poems and preserve a lively individuality for the subject. She then uses these précis to help detect the main themes for her project. Here is an example of such a “poetic transcription,” one she calls “The Gravity of Age”:

In The Long Week-End Wilfred Bion focuses on his emotional distress during the first twenty-one years of his life. Rarely employing psychoanalytic terminology, he attempts to explore his emotional experience directly, struggling to find a language that could capture the complexity of inner life. Central to The Long Week-End is his desire to communicate how easily he could feel unintelligent, inept, weak, ashamed, guilty, angry, frightened, and cowardly in various settings. This self-criticism at times undermines his theoretical stance in this work, as he emphasizes the importance of doubt and uncertainty when attempting to comprehend most dimensions of experience.

Carol Coutu  

Personally moving and deeply affecting, this panel was not typical psychoanalytic fare. Its topic—working with patients who are severely ill and confronting death—is infrequently discussed in the literature. Intimate accounts by analysts concerning the feelings and memories evoked in them when working with dying patients are rarely presented in public forums. As Theodore Jacobs stated in his discussion, “We are accustomed to working with patients struggling with problems of living, not dying.” We tend to shy away from the subject of death and thoughts of our own mortality. Yet, death is inevitable and we need to begin thinking and talking more about it. This panel stands as a sensitive, illuminating, and thought-provoking introduction to an important topic that has been given insufficient attention.

The panel, introduced by moderator Caryle Perlman, explored the ways in which patients’ illnesses affect the analyst and the analytic work. Each panelist, in his or her unique manner, revealed an ongoing inner turmoil while working for months or years with a severely ill and possibly dying patient. In their presentations, the panelists moved back and forth between past and present, personal and professional, philosophical and clinical. Their recollections included tributes to revered authors and memories of emotionally laden life events. Questions concerning analytic technique and doubts about professional competence were infused with sadness concerning past losses and fears of eventual death. Self-examination, at times quite painful, was ongoing for all the panelists.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 21, 2017. Panelists: Caryle Perlman (moderator), Philip Herschenfeld, Judy L. Kantrowitz, Peggy E. Warren. Discussant: Theodore J. Jacobs.

Psychoanalytic Writing

Rensal the Redbit: A Psychoanalytic Fairy Tale. By Eugene J. Mahon. London: Karnac Books, 2015, 64 pp., $11.95 paperback.

Elena Balashova-Shamis

Elena Balashova-Shamis

13323 Washington Boulevard, Suite 102
Los Angeles, CA 90066
E-mail: drelenab@gmail.com

Elena Balashova-Shamis   Faculty, New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; faculty, Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies (LAISPS); volunteer faculty, University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA); faculty and dissertation advisor, Ryokan College of Psychology.

Eugene Mahon's Rensal the Redbit can arguably be compared to such classics as Saint-Exupéry's The Little Prince and Carroll's Alice in Wonderland, both of which were written for children and adults, or at least were intended to make contact with the adult's “inner child.” The two interlocutors of Mahon's story—Rensal, a small curious “redbit” (more later) and The Tall One, a grown-up wise one—meet on a regular basis and get involved in deep discussions about various philosophical and existential issues. Mahon draws our attention not just to the content of their conversations, but to the dynamics of their interactions and gradual changes in Rensal as the result of their relationship. Mahon invites us to join their conversations, which revolve around the central themes of the book: How does psychoanalysis work, and what happens during the psychoanalytic process? How does a mind develop, and how does a person grow? Mahon has of course investigated such issues in his wonderful formal papers, and to some extent I think this book may be viewed as a poetic summary of his work.

The book comprises sixteen chapters, whose rich metaphorical language and vivid imagery are sure to stimulate the reader's imagination and associations. It is impossible to overlook similarities with psychoanalytical process and concepts. In reading the book I was reminded of Ogden's thinking about different psychoanalytic perspectives being much like different languages. Despite the overlap of semantic content, each language creates additional meaning that cannot be generated by the other languages. The interpreter becomes an active preserver and creator of meaning (Ogden 1990). While reading Rensal, I often found certain passages triggering concepts from Klein, Bion, Winnicot, Ogden, or Freud, Other readers could easily hear the echoes of other analytic theories.

Nathan Kravis on Shulman's “The Analyst's Pleasure”

Nathan Kravis

Nathan Kravis

141 East 55th Street, Suite 2F
New York, NY 10022
E-mail: nkravis57@gmail.com

Nathan Kravis  

I welcome the opportunity to respond to Michael Shulman's “‘Unavoidable Satisfactions’: The Analyst's Pleasure” (JAPA 64/4), in which he devotes considerable space to a critique of my article “The Analyst's Hatred of Analysis” (Kravis 2013a).

Shulman, claiming that pleasure “is a relatively unambiguous datum” (p. 698 n. 2), considers the problematizing of pleasure in analytic work mostly “unnecessary,” as well as “misleading and destructive” (p. 697). He views attempts like mine to describe analysts' ambivalence about analysis as hackneyed posturing rather than part of a long-standing tradition of self-reflection on analytic work that might contribute something worthwhile.

Where I see ambivalence, Shulman diagnoses anxiety among analysts—anxiety about feeling and expressing pleasure in their work. He portrays papers like mine as fostering this anxiety and contributing to a misunderstanding of the nature of analytic work. Toward this end he caricatures my article as depicting analytic work as unremittingly “toilsome and painful” (p. 703). In support of this view he cites a few critical comments from two of the four commentaries on my paper, those by Jacobs (2013) and Robin (2013), but ignores the more laudatory ones by Moss (2013) and Soni (2013), along with my reply to all four (Kravis 2013b). Cherry-picking of this kind to make a point is not uncommon, but in this case the result is a mischaracterization of my paper as relentlessly negative, as if I view analytic work as horribly masochistic. In reality my paper focuses not on masochism but on the analyst's narcissistic balance in the face of the demands of the analytic situation. My paper attempts to give fuller voice to the ambivalence arising from these demands, not to present a starkly negative picture of analytic work. Beginning with the first sentence of my abstract, I speak throughout my paper of both love and hate.

Shulman presents my views as both reprising a tiresome cliché of dutiful analytic drudgery and as suspiciously idiosyncratic. But he can't have it both ways.

Golan Shahar and Linda Mayes

800 Ben-Gurion Avenue

ISRAEL
E-mail: shaharg@bgu.ac.il

Linda Mayes  

A number of broad-based, theoretical-philosophical tenets are to be gleaned from Sidney Blatt's voluminous, extensive, and unique scholarly work. Dominant themes in Blatt's life and work include (1) his fascination with cognition, particularly mental maps, or representations, of reality; (2) the influence of humanistic values (e.g., agency, need for growth, gestalts, need for balance, and an optimistic focus on resilience); and (3) a steadfast adherence to Freud's revolutionary discovery of the dynamic unconscious. Philosophically speaking, Blatt has left a unique, integrative, and vibrant legacy for the field, one that might be called cognitive-humanistic psychodynamics.

Mattias Desmet  

Transference implies the actualization of the analyst in the analytic encounter. Lacan developed this idea through the syntagm presence of the analyst. In the course of his seminars, however, two completely different presences emerge, with major implications for how the treatment is directed. In the light of Lacan's idea that the transference is constituted in Real, Symbolic, and Imaginary dimensions, it can be seen how in his early work the analyst's presence is a phenomenon at the crossroads between signifiers and images. From the 1960s onward, however, the analyst's presence comes to necessarily involve the Real. This means it points to the moment at which symbolization reaches its limits. The clinical implications of this later interpretation of the presence of the analyst as incorporating the Real are manifold and affect psychoanalytic practice with regard to the position and the interventions of the analyst. Specifically, interventions targeted at provoking changes in defenses against experiences of excess or senselessness are discussed and illustrated with case vignettes and a published case. With transference considered “the navel of the treatment,” the necessity that traumatic material will emerge in relation to the analyst becomes clear.

Marcia Cavell   Associate Professor of Philosophy Emerita, Purchase College, State University of New York; CORST graduate, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

Man is the only animal that blushes, Mark Twain remarked (1897, p. 256). We blush when we are embarrassed, or worse, mortified, and when we are feeling ashamed. Shame is both more primitive than guilt—the toddler can feel shame well before she emerges from the oedipal crisis—and more mature. For while guilt can direct one's attention to those one has wronged and who now demand reparation, Bernard Williams (2008)

152

writes, “it cannot by itself help one to understand one's relations to those happenings, or to rebuild a self that has done these things and the world in which that self has to live. Only shame can do that, because it embodies conceptions of what one is and how one is related to others” (p. 93). The very capacity for shame testifies to an alert moral sense.

Of all the emotions, with the possible exception of love in its various forms, guilt has drawn the most attention from psychoanalysts. Its cousin, shame, was fairly neglected until the early seventies, when Piers and Singer published a new edition of Shame and Guilt, their landmark study of 1953. One of the factors contributing to the increased interest in shame among psychoanalysts, Akhtar writes in his introduction to Shame, a new volume of essays on the subject which he has edited and to which he is a contributor, “is that psychoanalytic clinicians are treating more patients whose personality organization, emanating from devastating injuries and humiliations during childhood, is not along the tripartite model of the mind (id-ego-superego) and whose anxieties are less related to instinct-morality conflicts than to the dread of exposure and narcissistic mortification” (p. xix). Akhtar does not comment on why this might be so.

The structure of Shame parallels that of Akhtar's recent Greed: both books bring together a number of essays, primarily by well-known psychoanalysts, that are grouped together under the headings “Developmental Realm,” “Cultural Realm,” and “Clinical Realm.” For the best introduction in Shame to some of the fundamental issues in the subject, I suggest the reader do what I am going to do and begin not at the beginning but with the fourth essay, “The Cultural Faces of Shame,” by Apurvah Shah.

Daniel Benveniste's Interwoven Lives tells the life story of W. Ernest Freud, born Ernst Halberstadt, and his relationship with his famous grandfather, Sigmund Freud, and his aunt, Anna Freud. The book spans a century of psychoanalytic history, from the dawn of psychoanalysis preceding Ernst's birth in 1914, through his death in 2008. Benveniste draws the reader into a deep and complex study of the least known of the three Freuds, Ernst, whose life unfolded against the backdrop of the loss of his most significant attachments: the larger-than-life figures of his grandfather and Aunt Anna and his mother, Sophie Halberstadt (née Freud), who died when he was quite young.

Interwoven Lives is a scholarly and meticulously researched labor of love—for the author's psychoanalytic forebears, for psychoanalysis, and indeed for Benveniste's main subject, Ernst, the only grandchild of Sigmund Freud to become a psychoanalyst. The book offers an alluring view of the golden era of psychoanalysis. Peopled by the heroes and heroines of our field, the book offers a window onto a period of remarkable psychological breakthroughs, unfolding against the backdrop of one of the darkest moments in history. Its discoveries, its nuances, and the stories that emerge shine a light on the foundational elements of what we now know as psychoanalysis. Indeed, so rich and engrossing are Benveniste's anecdotes and analyses of the Freud family that as I wrote this review I had to refrain from giving away too much of the story!

Interwoven Lives serves multiple purposes: it is a psychological portrait of a life of extremes, an historical overview of the new field, an examination of a complex sociopolitical era, and a study of the relationships among members of the Freud family.

This slim volume is a fine representative of the work being done by Continental, especially Italian, psychoanalysts working within post-Bionian field theory. We are shown the analyst's mind at work as we enter the realm of Mauro Manica's idiosyncratic version of field theory, a work pushing the boundaries separating psychoanalytic writing, literary creation, and philosophical speculation. Manica's work gives us an entrancing example

560

of the clinical, aesthetic, and philosophical thinking that can be generated within the porously boundaried metapsychology of field theory.

The author's central interest is the development of the self within a relational matrix, and the impasses and challenges of such creation. Winnicott even more than Bion is the essential master and inspiration for Manica's description of early mental life, though he also refers to Laplanche's extension of Winnicottian ideas, which often sounds like Winnicott's description of the early environment of the infant-mother pair. Like Winnicott (and inspired as well by the frequently mentioned Meltzer, Grotstein, Bromberg, and Ogden, and even more closely by Ferro and Civitarese), Manica is most passionately focused on an understanding of how the self is constituted, how early mental life, inevitably both relational and instinctual, creates a self—or fails to—within its interpenetrating relational environment. Indeed, this work is as much about how to understand selfhood as it is about psychoanalytic technique or treatment, which takes second place, in this volume, to using psychoanalytic understanding to construct the self and to interrogate and define the traumatic events capable of abrogating this process.

And what sort of psychoanalytic understanding? In addition to his grounding in Winnicott and Bion, Manica is inspired by Freud, Jung, Klein, Kohut, Lacan, and Laplanche and uses elements of all these theorists' work without much concern for disagreements and inconsistencies among them. I don't think Manica would consider this a criticism, as in a sense he is sharing with us his own inspirations, his own thickly layered, messy, fecund, associative mind. He acknowledges that he selects freely among theorists, as he does from among artistic works, in populating his internal world. In that sense there is something very personal and supremely individualistic about his writing, a quality which may or may not appeal to the reader, but which is clearly intentional.

In China: A New Beginning for Psychoanalysis, Teresa Yuan, an Argentinian training and supervising analyst of Chinese heritage, narrates her efforts to support the development of psychoanalysis in China. Her journey spans more than two decades, during which she has been a driving force behind the acceptance of psychoanalysis in some of the most important mental health centers and hospitals in China. She was actively developing psychoanalytic training programs at a time when psychoanalytic private practices in psychotherapy were unknown in the country. Yuan's book is testimony to the pioneering work of IPA psychoanalysts in China since the 1980s, work supporting the growth of what soon will be the fourth region of the IPA.

Yuan was born in Argentina of Chinese and Syrian immigrants. Her father, a Chinese immigrant, instilled in her a passion for her Chinese roots. Her identification with the paternal side of her family led her to embark on a journey to spread psychoanalytic thinking by teaching in China. As a candidate, her passion for psychoanalysis and for Chinese culture drove her to sit on several IPA committees promoting the development of psychoanalysis in her father's homeland. Yuan, a psychologist trained at the University of Buenos Aires, has published extensively at the intersection of psychoanalysis and Chinese culture.

In her writing, Yuan integrates Chinese folk tales, philosophy, and fables to creatively illustrate the different nuances in the way Chinese culture represents, understands, and treats mental health. She opens the book with the evocative poem “Ode to the Cherry Tree Blossom,” by Mao Tsetung. She describes the Mei, a variety of cherry tree that bloooms in the winter.

In Insanity and Sanctity in Byzantium, Youval Rotman shows how the Hellenistic and Jewish traditions intersected with the emerging Christian tradition and culture, which led to new forms of religious communities under Byzantine rule. He focuses here on figures who displayed extreme forms of behavior in that period: the holy fool, the ascetic, and the martyr. Their sanctification rendered their abnormalities functional in society, and shaped in complex ways the transition from paganism to Christianity.

Youval Rotman, a social historian of the ancient and early medieval Eastern Mediterranean world, taught in the history department at Yale before becoming Associate Professor of Jewish History at Tel Aviv University.

He begins this book with a study of insanity, locating his considerations in the shift in its definition in the 1960s and 1970s. This was the time of my own psychiatric and psychoanalytic training. I recall being very taken with the views of Michel Foucault on insanity, asylums, and prisons. I was influenced also by Thomas Szasz's book The Myth of Mental Illness: Foundations of a Theory of Personal Conflict (1961). Although Rotman does not cite him, Szasz too opposed the tendency to label people “disabled by living” as mentally ill, believing that psychological problems should not be classified as diseases.

Karl Menninger (I trained at the Menninger School of Psychiatry) exchanged letters with Szasz after publication of the latter's Insanity: The Idea and Its Consequences (1987). In the letters both questioned the value of psychiatric diagnoses and supported the view that mental illness is an existential condition, not a medical one. I recall Karl's Saturday morning seminars at MSP in which he made it very clear that referring to a person as a schizophrenic was verboten.

Hanni Mann-Shalvi's From Ultrasound to Army is an important and original book, its title one of the most arresting in recent years. We know of nothing like the book in scope and ambition, as it examines the development of Jewish Israeli boys and men from conception to young adulthood. Mann-Shalvi's original research, conducted fourteen years ago in fulfillment of a doctorate in psychology and several years before qualifying in psychoanalysis, focuses on the parenting experience of a group of twelve couples studied intensively. She supplements her understanding of problems in the development of their sons by drawing on her current clinical experience as a psychoanalyst treating adolescents and young adults presenting the kind of developmental problems identified in her research. She draws heavily on other research into trauma and development, studies of Israeli and worldwide war trauma and its sequelae, her far-reaching knowledge of Israeli culture, and the psychoanalytic and developmental literatures, in order to convey the sweep of issues concerning masculine development in this war-torn and trauma-conscious country.

The book is divided into two major parts, the first presenting the author's basic research and the second on development and family dynamics; a third part provides a short coda on psychoanalytic interventions ameliorating the trauma of men who have grown up in a war-conscious society in which they have always expected to become soldiers. The book's first section could usefully have been 50 percent shorter and would have read more easily. An editor's strong hand would have been useful here to avoid repetitiveness in the narrative. Nevertheless, the accumulated impact of intense, direct speech from the various couples yields a convincing picture of the wearing emotional life of the parents of Israeli sons.

In Part 1, Mann-Shalvi sets the scene by recounting her own profoundly distressed experience of sending her eldest son off to the army. After eighteen years of barely recognized anticipatory anxiety, she realizes that “the moment of his mobilization had accompanied me all the while I was raising him” (p. 2). Having been drawn to this study by this personal connection to the topic, she uses her experience to connect and empathize with the couples under study, which adds resonance to the themes. Mann-Shalvi discusses the validity of qualitative research and presents her choice of sample and method of gathering data by engaging in free-form interviews, then binning material to collect themes.

The Work of the Negative is a difficult book, hard to read, infused with Continental philosophy and Freudian metapsychology, containing almost no clinical material. Yet, in my view and that of others, it is of vital importance to psychoanalysis, both theoretically and clinically. Green's writing style can be infuriating; like Freud, he wanders from subject to subject, rarely providing a coherent outline of his thinking, assuming an intimate acquaintance with Freud, with Lacan, and with Hegel, among others, asking the reader to form conclusions and pull together clinical implications. French psychoanalysis has always been in dialogue with French philosophy, and it would be important for the reader to have some familiarity with Hegel, Merleau-Ponty, Ricoeur, and Sartre. One could skip the chapter on Hegel, but the rest of the book is deeply imbued with philosophical terminology. In dialogue with Freud, especially his aborted beginnings and uncompleted late thoughts, Green aims to complete Freud's project, suggesting where he thinks Freud might have gone. I do not think Green presents a finished theory in this book; rather, he is still reaching and trying out different points of view. Freud never fully developed a theory of objects, and this is Green's central focus, the interplay of external and internal objects, internal objects as representations, and their various uses within the psyche.

There have been, I believe, three revolutionary turns in psychoanalytic theory. The first, of course, occurred with Sigmund Freud. His theory is largely intrapsychic, focusing on psychic conflict, repression as the chief method of inhibiting instincts, and symptoms resulting from the return of the repressed. Of particular importance to Green is the concept of representation: Freud uses Darstellung1

1Both Darstellung and Vorstellung were translated by Strachey as “representation,” thus blurring the distinction between the concepts.1119

Comings and Goings

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

Once again it is time to bid good-bye to JAPA Editorial Board members rotating off the board and to welcome new members joining or returning to us. We thank the following Editorial Board members, who contributed greatly during the past three years and who rotated off the board as of December 31, 2016: Charles P. Fisher, William H. Gottdiener, Adrienne Harris, Lewis A. Kirshner, and K. Lynne Moritz. We also thank Angelica Kaner, whose term as an Editorial Associate has concluded.

It is with deep sadness that I announce the passing of our colleague Richard Gottlieb, who died just as this issue was going to press. In his fourteen years as an Associate Editor of the journal, his commitment to scholarship and his gentle guidance were a treasured resource. To the many authors he worked with he was more mentor and teacher than editor or reviewer. He will be missed.

We look forward to working with colleagues appointed to the Editorial Board for the term January 1, 2017, through December 31, 2019: Phyllis Ackman, John S. Auerbach, John P. Muller, Jeffrey Prager, Avgi Saketopoulou, and Golan Shahar. We also welcome Benjamin J. Herbstman as a new Editorial Associate and Nathan Kravis, who joins us as an Associate Editor.

There is also “comings and goings” news in relation to translations of JAPA articles. We thank Sunghae Min, our dedicated colleague and translator, who has turned over Korean translations to Byung Geon Choi, assisted by Kyung Soon Park and Hamin Lee. We also welcome a new Mandarin translator, Xu Yuancong, a psychotherapist in Shanghai. In addition, we are pleased to announce a new collaborative project with Gustavo Jarast, Scientific Chair of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association, who has arranged for the translation of recent JAPA articles in a Spanish-language annual to appear in 2017. We are grateful to those who translate JAPA articles, thereby making them more accessible to our global audience.

Lastly, we are pleased to announce that our continuing education initiative was launched on Monday, November 21, 2016, making CE credits available for M.D.s, psychologists, and social workers. The link may be found on APsaA's website under Resources. We thank Brian Canty, Chris Broughton, and Carolyn Gatto for their hard work in bringing this project to fruition.

Murray Schwartz   Professor Emeritus, Emerson College; Scholar Member and Coordinator, Center for Multidisciplinary Psychoanalytic Studies (COMPASS), Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

In “The Question of Lay Analysis” (1926), Freud sketched an ideal curriculum for psychoanalytic training that “would include branches of knowledge which are remote from medicine and which the doctor does not come across in his practice: the history of civilization, mythology, the psychology of religion and the science of literature” (p. 246). Marilyn Charles, an analyst at the Austen Riggs Center in Stockbridge, Massachusetts, has made Freud's hope central to her writings. A prolific author, Charles has written eloquently about metaphor, myth, and the constructions of reality embedded in the stories we tell for and about ourselves. She is also a master teacher of therapeutically useful versions of theoretical concepts derived from Grotstein, Bion, Lacan, Matte-Blanco, and Winnicott, her guiding beacons in the practice of repairing broken life narratives. As President of Division 39 of the American Psychological Association, she promoted the central value of the humanities in psychoanalytic education and practice. Although Freud's curriculum also retained medical and biological training, Charles often opposes psychiatric categorization and the use of chemical interventions. For her the analytic process is a work of interpersonal transformation, the analyst a midwife to the birth of individual autonomy and authenticity.

Literature and Psychoanalysis: The Stories We Live gathers sixteen chapters, most of them amended versions of papers previously published in various psychoanalytic journals.

Drawing on her two fields of expertise, as a professor of philosophy and a psychoanalyst at the department of psychoanalysis at the University of Paris-VIII, Clotilde Leguil reviews in her recent book, Being and Gender, Man/Woman after Lacan, the current approaches to gender and sexuality taken by philosophical feminism and psychoanalysis. On related subjects, she has published Les amoureuses: Voyage au bout de la féminité (2009) and In Treatment: Lost in Therapy (2013). Leguil's latest book anchors gender issues in their historical, philosophical, psychoanalytic, and aesthetic context. The multiple facets of her argument avoid the pitfalls of a radically unilateral approach to this complex topic.

As the book begins, Leguil underscores the difference between gender studies and the gender question in psychoanalysis. For gender studies, gender is a normative cultural construction that oppresses identity.

The Analyst's Solitude and Search for Truth

Errancias: Freud y Lacan en los pagos de san José de Mayo (Wanderings: Freud and Lacan in the region of San José de Mayo). By Daniel Gil. Montevideo: Ediciones Trilce, 2011, 213 pp.

Marie-Ange Pongis-Khandjian

Marie-Ange Pongis-Khandjian

1180 Murray Avenue
Quebec (QC) G1S 3B6
CANADA
E-mail: m-a.pongis-khandjian@crchudequebec.ulaval.ca

Marie-Ange Pongis-Khandjian   Psychologist and psychoanalyst, Quebec Psychoanalytic Society.

Preoccupied with the transmission of psychoanalysis, Daniel Gil, an Uruguayan psychoanalyst, wanted to create an anthology solely about psychoanalytic theory and practice and based on writings he has published over a career of thirty years. As is often the case, the road to hell was paved with good intentions: he realized it was impossible to refrain from engaging in constant dialogue with the voices of philosophy.

The appealing and catchy title of this book of more than two hundred pages, to be savored in small doses, invites the reader to wander through “dreaming thoughts” (Pontalis 2000), reflections, hypotheses, worries, and life experiences and opens up paths to one's own free associations.

Gil notes that he has had no formal training in philosophy but grew up listening to the lively discussions of adults in his home town of San José de Mayo.1 When reading the texts he has written over the years, it is nonetheless obvious that this domain has been a fertile field of exploration and reflection for him. His great erudition gives readers an appetite for expanding their own knowledge.

Errancias is divided into three parts. The first, “The Analyst's Place,” presents an atopia,2 a space of placelessness made of solitude between silence and speech, a twilight zone, a land of both shadow and substance, of things and ideas, allowing both withdrawal and openness, a dramatic experience of ephemeral brilliance, a symbolic wandering along an arduous path “made by walking”3 between desire, freedom, and death.

1Founded in 1783, the town is located in southern Uruguay, fifty-six miles from the center of Montevideo.2Formed by the combination of an alpha privative and the Greek topos, meaning place, position, location.3See Antonio Machado's lines “Traveler, there is no path. / The path is made by walking” (1912).595

Humphrey Morris   Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

What place does this book stake out for itself as a late arrival in the vast and densely populated world of Freud studies? Hasn't everything already been said? Paul Schimmel bases Sigmund Freud's Discovery of Psychoanalysis on his reading of the Standard Edition and of Freud's published correspondance, and his secondary sources are all well-known works of biography and intellectual history by writers like Anzieu, Breger, Clark, Ellenberger, Gay, Jones, Makari, Malcolm, Sulloway, and Wollheim. So he has taken on a challenge, and it is one he succeeds in meeting: he claims our interest as readers with his own attentive interest, as a practicing analyst, in Freud's mind. The terrain is familiar, from Freud's early neurological research to the Charcot year to the Project to Studies on Hysteria to Fliess and the seduction theory to the Irma dream and then, with a jump, to “Mourning and Melancholia” and Freud's disillusionment over the realities of the war. What is distinctive is Schimmel's generous, intelligent engagement with Freud's intellectual and emotional struggles, an engagement that is psychoanalytic in the best sense, for it leaves us room to rethink our own engagements with Freud.

927

Florence Guignard is a child psychoanalyst and a scholar. The author of Au vif de l'infantile (1996) and Epître à l'objet (1997), she has published more than 250 articles. A member of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society and the IPA, she founded, together with Annie Anzieu, the Association for the Psychoanalysis of the Child (APE) in 1984. Ten years later, in 1994, she founded the European Society for the Psychoanalysis of the Child and the Adolescent (SEPEA).

Quelle psychanalyse pour le XXIe siècle? is the first of a two-volume project. In these 260 pages, Guignard conveys her passion for psychoanalysis and a deep commitment to developing psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice. She approaches theory and practice as together an enduring science of the mind and as a treatment. This is a dense and meticulous work. The author writes from the perspective of her rigorous grounding in classical Freudian theory, particularly as expressed in French psychoanalytic theory and its emphasis on the drives.

Throughout the book's diverse chapters a few basic themes knit together the many ideas presented.

1126

I appreciate Nathan Kravis's responding to my paper, as well as the opportunity to reply. I trust that our exchange will not only demonstrate the differences in our viewpoints but will also direct continuing attention to the experience of analyzing.

I am concerned that Kravis begins with a basic misrepresentation of my argument. My argument is not, as he asserts, that because of the relatively unambiguous datum of the experience of pleasure, therefore the problematizing of the pleasure of analytic work is unnecessary. 조나단 리어  

웅변가 안티폰(Antiphon)은 기원전 5세기 후반에 살았던 인 물이다. 그는 기원전 411년 과두파의 쿠데타(oligarchical coup)에서, 아테네의 민주주의를 전복시키는 데에 결정적인 역할 을 한 자로 유명하다. 민주주의가 복구된 후, 안티폰은 반역죄로 재판에 처해 사형선고를 받는다.1 한편, 정계에 발을 들이기 전 안 티폰이 무슨 일을 하고 살았었는지는 상대적으로 덜 알려져 있는 데, 알고 보면 그는 일종의 정신분석가였다. 슈도-플루타르크 (Pseudo-Plutarch) (3-4세기 AD)는 영웅전(Lives of Ten Orators)에서 우리에게 다음과 같이 말한다.

안티폰이 아직 시(poetry)에 몰두할 무렵 그는 마음의 괴로움을 치료하는 방법을 개발했는데, 의사에게 신체질환을 치료받는 것과 다를 바 없는 방법이었다. 그는 코린트(Corinth) 아고라 근처에 개원을 하고는, 말을 통 해 괴로움을 낫게 하는 치료법을 발견했다는 내용의 광고를 냈다. 그리 고 그를 찾는 환자들의 괴로움의 원인을 탐구함으로 그는 그들의 고통을 경감시켰다. 그러나 그는 이 직업이 자신의 수준에 맞지 않는다고 여겨 웅변으로 진로를 바꾸었다. (833c-d)

나는 이 짤막한 일화가 재미있으면서도 의미심장하다. 이는, 인 간의 삶에는 정신분석 (내지는 그와 비슷한 그 무엇)을 자꾸만 찾 게끔 하는 압력이 내재된 동시에, 이 힘을 거부하는 내면화된 사회 적 압박이 함께 존재한다는 점을 시사한다. 정신치료보다 웅변이 보다 자신의 품위에 걸맞다는 결심은, 결국 법대에 진학하기로 마 음먹는 행보의 기원전 5세기 버젼이다.

오늘 나는 프로이트의 저술 내에서도 이와 유사한 정신분석의 외면이 발견된다는 점에 대해 이야기하고자 한다.

1투키디데스(Thucydides) (431-411 B.C.) 는 그에 대해 다음과 같이 기술했다: “그 사건 전체를 기획하고 이 참사가 터지게끔 길을 열며, 또한 이 일에 가장 많은 생각을 투자한 장본인은 바로 당대 아테네의 최고의 인물 중 하나인 안티폰이었다…” (The Peloponnesian WarVIII:68, p490)NP7

JAPA Prize Announcements

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.
